content.text: Continuous Monitoring of Third-Party AI Risk

A point-in-time TPRM assessment provides a snapshot of vendor risk. Continuous monitoring extends that snapshot into a live feed — detecting certification lapses, vulnerability disclosures, service degradations, model changes, and subprocessor additions between formal assessment cycles. For LLM vendors specifically, the risk landscape can shift overnight: a single CVE in an underlying GPU framework or a model update that alters safety behaviour can invalidate the controls you assessed six months ago.

: DORA Article 30 and the EBA ICT Risk Guidelines require organisations to maintain ongoing oversight of critical third-party providers — not just annual assessments. Regulators expect evidence of continuous monitoring in audit packages.

attrs.alt: Continuous Third-Party AI Risk Monitoring Architecture diagram

content.text: normalises and scores them per vendor and per domain, and the
triages severity and routes to the appropriate action track.
Each signal source covers a distinct risk dimension for LLM vendors:
SOC 2, ISO 27001, CSA STAR expiry and opinion changes
Published CVEs affecting vendor's disclosed tech stack
Critical CVE in GPU driver, inference runtime
Public AI incident reports mentioning the vendor
Safety failure, bias incident, data leak
Live synthetic requests to the vendor API
New cloud provider, new third-country transfer
Model version bump, capability change, safety update

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date, datetime
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional

class SignalKind(str, Enum):
    CERT_EXPIRY        = "cert_expiry"
    CERT_QUALIFIED     = "cert_qualified"      # auditor issued qualified opinion
    CVE_PUBLISHED      = "cve_published"
    INCIDENT_REPORTED  = "incident_reported"   # vendor self-report or AI Incident DB
    SLA_BREACH         = "sla_breach"
    SUBPROCESSOR_ADDED = "subprocessor_added"
    MODEL_CHANGED      = "model_changed"
    STATUS_DEGRADED    = "status_degraded"

class SignalSeverity(str, Enum):
    INFO     = "info"
    LOW      = "low"
    MODERATE = "moderate"
    HIGH     = "high"
    CRITICAL = "critical"

@dataclass
class MonitoringSignal:
    vendor_id: str
    kind: SignalKind
    severity: SignalSeverity
    title: str
    detail: str
    detected_at: datetime = field(default_factory=datetime.utcnow)
    source_url: Optional[str] = None

    def is_actionable(self) -> bool:
        return self.severity in (SignalSeverity.MODERATE, SignalSeverity.HIGH,
                                 SignalSeverity.CRITICAL)

from datetime import date, timedelta

@dataclass
class CertRecord:
    vendor_id: str
    cert_name: str              # e.g. "SOC 2 Type II"
    issued_date: date
    expiry_date: date
    report_url: Optional[str] = None
    qualified: bool = False     # True if auditor issued a qualified opinion

    def days_until_expiry(self) -> int:
        return (self.expiry_date - date.today()).days

class CertTracker:
    """Polls a certificate registry and emits signals when certs are near expiry."""

    WARNING_DAYS = 60   # emit MODERATE signal
    CRITICAL_DAYS = 14  # emit CRITICAL signal

    def __init__(self, certs: list[CertRecord]):
        self._certs = certs

    def scan(self) -> list[MonitoringSignal]:
        signals = []
        for cert in self._certs:
            days = cert.days_until_expiry()

            if cert.qualified:
                signals.append(MonitoringSignal(
                    vendor_id=cert.vendor_id,
                    kind=SignalKind.CERT_QUALIFIED,
                    severity=SignalSeverity.HIGH,
                    title=f"{cert.cert_name} — qualified opinion",
                    detail="Auditor issued a qualified opinion. Re-assessment required.",
                ))
            elif days <= 0:
                signals.append(MonitoringSignal(
                    vendor_id=cert.vendor_id,
                    kind=SignalKind.CERT_EXPIRY,
                    severity=SignalSeverity.CRITICAL,
                    title=f"{cert.cert_name} expired {-days} days ago",
                    detail=f"Certificate expired on {cert.expiry_date}. Control gap active.",
                ))
            elif days <= self.CRITICAL_DAYS:
                signals.append(MonitoringSignal(
                    vendor_id=cert.vendor_id,
                    kind=SignalKind.CERT_EXPIRY,
                    severity=SignalSeverity.CRITICAL,
                    title=f"{cert.cert_name} expires in {days} days",
                    detail="Obtain renewal certificate before expiry.",
                ))
            elif days <= self.WARNING_DAYS:
                signals.append(MonitoringSignal(
                    vendor_id=cert.vendor_id,
                    kind=SignalKind.CERT_EXPIRY,
                    severity=SignalSeverity.MODERATE,
                    title=f"{cert.cert_name} expires in {days} days",
                    detail="Request renewal certificate from vendor.",
                ))
        return signals
Canary probes send a known benign prompt on a schedule and validate that the response is structurally correct and within the latency SLA. They detect degradation before end users do:

import httpx
from datetime import datetime

@dataclass
class CanaryResult:
    vendor_id: str
    endpoint: str
    latency_ms: float
    status_code: int
    response_valid: bool
    checked_at: datetime = field(default_factory=datetime.utcnow)

class APICanaryProbe:
    """
    Sends a known benign prompt to a vendor API endpoint and checks:
    - HTTP status code
    - Response latency against SLA threshold
    - Structural validity of the response (non-empty, expected fields)
    """
    def __init__(self, vendor_id: str, endpoint: str,
                 api_key: str, latency_sla_ms: float = 3000):
        self.vendor_id = vendor_id
        self.endpoint  = endpoint
        self._headers  = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {api_key}"}
        self._sla_ms   = latency_sla_ms

    def probe(self) -> tuple[CanaryResult, list[MonitoringSignal]]:
        signals = []
        start = datetime.utcnow()
        try:
            resp = httpx.post(
                self.endpoint,
                headers=self._headers,
                json={"model": "production", "messages": [{"role": "user",
                      "content": "Reply with the single word: CANARY"}]},
                timeout=10,
            )
            latency = (datetime.utcnow() - start).total_seconds() * 1000
            valid = (resp.status_code == 200 and
                     "CANARY" in resp.json().get("choices", [{}])[0]
                                            .get("message", {}).get("content", ""))
        except Exception as exc:
            latency = (datetime.utcnow() - start).total_seconds() * 1000
            resp = type("R", (), {"status_code": 0})()
            valid = False
            signals.append(MonitoringSignal(
                vendor_id=self.vendor_id, kind=SignalKind.STATUS_DEGRADED,
                severity=SignalSeverity.CRITICAL,
                title="API canary probe failed with exception",
                detail=str(exc),
            ))

        result = CanaryResult(self.vendor_id, self.endpoint, latency,
                              resp.status_code, valid)

        if resp.status_code not in (200, 201) and resp.status_code != 0:
            signals.append(MonitoringSignal(
                vendor_id=self.vendor_id, kind=SignalKind.STATUS_DEGRADED,
                severity=SignalSeverity.HIGH,
                title=f"API returned HTTP {resp.status_code}",
                detail=f"Endpoint: {self.endpoint}",
            ))
        elif latency > self._sla_ms:
            signals.append(MonitoringSignal(
                vendor_id=self.vendor_id, kind=SignalKind.SLA_BREACH,
                severity=SignalSeverity.MODERATE,
                title=f"Latency {latency:.0f}ms exceeds SLA {self._sla_ms:.0f}ms",
                detail=f"Endpoint: {self.endpoint}",
            ))
        return result, signals
The aggregator assigns numeric weights to signal severity and computes a per-vendor aggregate score. Crossing a threshold triggers the corresponding action track:
KRIs translate raw signals into board-level metrics. Define thresholds and owners for each:
Days between notification and risk review
Vendors past due date for scheduled assessment
Days from vendor CVE disclosure to confirmed patch
Not every signal warrants the same response. Route alerts to the right owner at the right speed:
Vendor Manager → Risk Steward if unresolved

LLM model updates are uniquely risky: the API contract (endpoints, parameters) may be unchanged while the model's output distribution, safety guardrails, and capability boundaries shift significantly. Treat model changes as a first-class monitoring event:

content: : parse RSS/Atom feeds or webhook notifications from vendor status pages.
: maintain a fixed set of 20–50 canonical prompt-response pairs. On each model change notification, re-run the set and flag any response that deviates beyond a cosine-similarity threshold.
: include adversarial prompts in the canary set and verify the new model still refuses them. A model update that weakens safety guardrails is a material risk event.
: if the vendor changes the model without the contractual notice period, file a contractual exception and re-assess the model-change notice clause.

content.text: Continuous monitoring signals must feed back into the organisation's risk register — not remain siloed in the monitoring tool. When a vendor's aggregate score crosses the
Retrieve all risk register entries that cite this vendor as the source of a risk.
Set the control status for controls provided by this vendor to 'under review'.
Recalculate residual risk scores with control effectiveness temporarily reduced.
Notify the risk owner to review the entry and determine whether the inherent risk has changed.
CVE signals filtered by vendor tech stack

content: : signals that have no defined response time become noise. Every severity level needs an owner and a response deadline.
: vendors are contractually obligated to notify you of incidents, but delays happen. Run independent canary probes and subscribe to external sources (AI Incident Database, CVE feeds).
: a vendor may update system prompts, safety classifiers, or context-window handling without bumping the model version. Include behavioural regression canaries, not just version-string checks.
: low-severity INFO signals that fire constantly train teams to ignore the dashboard. Suppress INFO-level signals from real-time channels; aggregate them into a weekly digest instead.
: monitoring that escalates to CRITICAL needs a pre-prepared contingency plan. Without an exit plan, even a well-detected risk cannot be acted upon quickly.

content.text: Continuous monitoring converts the annual TPRM assessment into a living risk signal. Instrument six categories of signal source — certifications, API health, CVEs, AI incidents, subprocessor changes, and model changes — aggregate them with severity-weighted scoring per vendor, and route alerts to the right owner within defined SLAs. Tie the monitoring output back to the risk register so that a deteriorating vendor score automatically surfaces as an increased residual risk for every dependent AI use case. The goal is not zero alerts, but zero surprises: every material vendor risk event should be detected and triaged before it becomes a regulatory finding or a production incident.
